mnemonics: ARRAY TREE: A Root Really Yearns To Reach Every Element (for array representation). LINKED LOGIC: Left Link, Right Link Keeps Data Organized (for linked representation).
keyPoints: A binary tree can be represented using an array or a linked structure.
In an array representation, for a node at index 'i', its left child is at 2i+1 and its right child is at 2i+2.
In a linked representation, each node is an object containing data, a pointer to the left child, and a pointer to the right child.
Array representation is efficient for complete binary trees but can waste memory for sparse trees.
Linked representation is memory efficient for any tree shape but requires extra space for pointers.
commonMistakes: Off-by-one errors in array indexing (e.g., using 2i and 2i+1 instead of 2i+1 and 2i+2 for a 0-based index).
Forgetting to set the left and right pointers of a new node to NULL, which can lead to errors when traversing.
Assuming an array representation is always better because it avoids pointer overhead, ignoring the potential for wasted space in non-complete trees.
